title: Gas Station
description: Determine starting gas station to complete circular route

examples.explanation: Start checking station 0: total = 1-3 = -2 → fails immediately. Station 1: total = 2-4 = -2 → fails immediately. Station 2: total = 3-5 = -2 → fails immediately. Station 3: total = 4-1 = 3; next station 4: total = 3 + (5-2) = 6; next station 0: total = 6 + (1-3) = 4; next station 1: total = 4 + (2-4) = 2; next station 2: total = 2 + (3-5) = 0; completed all 5 stations with total ≥ 0 → valid start at 3.

Station 0: total = 2-3 = -1 → fails. Station 1: total = 3-4 = -1 → fails. Station 2: total = 4-3 = 1; next station 0: total = 1 + (2-3) = 0; next station 1: total = 0 + (3-4) = -1 → fails. All stations checked, none valid → return -1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Try every station as a starting point, simulate the circular route by checking if cumulative gas minus cost stays non-negative at each step. Use if no constraints on time, but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Gas Station Solution
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking every possible starting station for the circular route
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the gas station problem by systematically testing each station as a potential starting point. The intuition is straightforward: if we start at station i, we need to ensure that as we travel through all n stations in circular order, our gas tank never drops below zero. For each candidate start, we simulate the entire circular journey, tracking cumulative gas minus cost at each step. The algorithm works because it exhaustively checks all possibilities - if a valid starting point exists, this method will find it. However, this comes at the cost of O(n²) time complexity since for each of n starting points, we check n stations. The space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the station validation check - L0 orchestrates calling this validation for every possible starting station. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling the circular nature correctly (forgetting to wrap around), 2) Off-by-one errors in indices, 3) Not resetting cumulative gas for each new starting point, 4) Returning too early without checking all stations. To avoid these, carefully manage indices with modulo operations and ensure complete simulation for each candidate. Real code pattern: def canCompleteCircuit(gas, cost): n = len(gas); for start in range(n): total = 0; for i in range(n): station = (start + i) % n; total += gas[station] - cost[station]; if total < 0: break; if i == n-1 and total >= 0: return start; return -1

examples.trace: Start checking station 0: total = 1-3 = -2 → fails immediately. Station 1: total = 2-4 = -2 → fails immediately. Station 2: total = 3-5 = -2 → fails immediately. Station 3: total = 4-1 = 3; next station 4: total = 3 + (5-2) = 6; next station 0: total = 6 + (1-3) = 4; next station 1: total = 4 + (2-4) = 2; next station 2: total = 2 + (3-5) = 0; completed all 5 stations with total ≥ 0 → valid start at 3.

Station 0: total = 2-3 = -1 → fails. Station 1: total = 3-4 = -1 → fails. Station 2: total = 4-3 = 1; next station 0: total = 1 + (2-3) = 0; next station 1: total = 0 + (3-4) = -1 → fails. All stations checked, none valid → return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Validate Starting Station
ladders.desc: Check if a specific starting station can complete the circular route
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force solution - it determines whether starting at a particular station allows completion of the full circular journey. The intuition is simple: starting with an empty tank, we accumulate (gas - cost) at each station visited in order. If at any point the cumulative total becomes negative, the journey fails because we've run out of gas. If we complete all n stations with non-negative total, the starting station is valid. The algorithm: given start index, total = 0; for i from 0 to n-1: station = (start + i) % n; total += gas[station] - cost[station]; if total < 0: return false; return true. Time complexity is O(n) for checking one station, space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being called for each possible starting station. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to use modulo for circular traversal, 2) Not breaking early when total becomes negative, 3) Comparing against wrong threshold (should be < 0, not ≤ 0). Real code pattern: def isValidStart(start, gas, cost): total = 0; n = len(gas); for i in range(n): station = (start + i) % n; total += gas[station] - cost[station]; if total < 0: return False; return True

examples.trace: Station 3: total = 4-1 = 3; Station 4: total = 3 + (5-2) = 6; Station 0: total = 6 + (1-3) = 4; Station 1: total = 4 + (2-4) = 2; Station 2: total = 2 + (3-5) = 0; All stations visited with total ≥ 0 → valid.
Station 0: total = 2-3 = -1; Total negative at first station → immediately invalid.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cumulative Gas Tracking with Early Termination
ladders.desc: Track running total of (gas - cost) and stop when it becomes negative
ladders.explanation: This core logic represents the fundamental insight for checking route feasibility: we maintain a running total of (gas - cost) as we traverse stations. The intuition is that gas represents fuel gained, cost represents fuel consumed to reach the next station. The difference (gas - cost) is the net change in our tank at each station. By keeping a cumulative sum, we can determine if we ever run out of gas (total < 0). The early termination optimization is crucial - once the total becomes negative, we know the route is impossible from that starting point, so we can stop checking further stations. This is what makes the validation subroutine O(n) rather than always completing all n checks. The algorithm: initialize total = 0; for each station in sequence: total += gas[i] - cost[i]; if total < 0: return false; return true. Time complexity for n stations is O(n), space O(1). This feeds into L1 as the main logic for station validation. Common mistakes: 1) Not resetting total between different starting points, 2) Using wrong comparison (should be < 0, not ≤ 0), 3) Continuing computation after total becomes negative. Real code pattern: total = 0; for i in range(n): total += gas[i] - cost[i]; if total < 0: return False; return True

examples.trace: Station 0: total = 4-2 = 2; Station 1: total = 2 + (1-3) = 0; Station 2: total = 0 + (5-2) = 3; Completed with total ≥ 0 → valid sequence.
Station 0: total = 2-3 = -1; Total negative immediately → invalid sequence.

ladders.title: Building Block — Circular Array Traversal with Modulo
ladders.desc: Traverse an array circularly starting from any index
ladders.explanation: This building block addresses the circular nature of the gas station problem. The intuition is that we need to visit stations in order, but when we reach the end of the array, we wrap around to the beginning to complete the circle. The modulo operator (%) provides an elegant solution: (start + i) % n gives us the correct station index for the i-th step from starting position 'start'. For example, with n=5 and start=3: i=0 → (3+0)%5=3, i=1 → (3+1)%5=4, i=2 → (3+2)%5=0, i=3 → (3+3)%5=1, i=4 → (3+4)%5=2. This creates a circular traversal. The algorithm: for i in range(n): index = (start + i) % n; process array[index]. Time complexity is O(n), space O(1). This feeds into L2 by enabling the cumulative tracking to work correctly for circular routes. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to use modulo and accessing out-of-bounds indices, 2) Using wrong formula (should be start+i not i alone), 3) Not handling the complete circle (missing last station). Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n): current_index = (start + i) % n; value = arr[current_index]; # process value

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Net Gas Change per Station
ladders.desc: Calculate gas[i] - cost[i] for each station
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the fundamental unit of the gas station problem: the net gas change at each station. The intuition is simple: at station i, we gain gas[i] amount of fuel, but we must spend cost[i] fuel to travel to the next station. The difference (gas[i] - cost[i]) tells us whether we have a surplus (positive) or deficit (negative) after visiting that station. This single value encapsulates the station's contribution to our journey. The algorithm is straightforward: for each index i, compute diff[i] = gas[i] - cost[i]. Time complexity is O(n) for n stations, space can be O(1) if computed on-the-fly or O(n) if stored. This feeds into L3 as the values we process during circular traversal. Common mistakes: 1) Subtracting in wrong order (cost[i] - gas[i]), 2) Not handling arrays of equal length, 3) Forgetting that indices must match between gas and cost arrays. Real code pattern: n = len(gas); for i in range(n): diff = gas[i] - cost[i]; # use diff in calculations

examples.trace: Station 0: 5-2=3; Station 1: 3-4=-1; Station 2: 7-3=4.
All stations: 4-4=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Iteration and Index Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Basic looping through arrays and calculating with indices
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the most basic operations needed for the gas station problem: iterating through arrays and performing arithmetic with indices. The intuition is that arrays store sequences of values that we can access using indices (positions). We use loops to visit each element in order. Index arithmetic involves calculations with these positions, like adding offsets or using modulo. For beginners: think of an array as a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2,... Each box holds a value. A loop lets us look inside each box one by one. When we calculate (start + i) % n, we're finding which box to look at when we want to start at position 'start' and move forward 'i' steps, wrapping around if we reach the end. The algorithm: initialize index; while condition: access array[index]; update index. Time depends on number of iterations, space O(1). This feeds into L4 as the mechanism for accessing gas and cost arrays. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Starting indices at 1 instead of 0, 2) Going out of array bounds, 3) Not updating loop variable correctly. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)): value = arr[i]; # process value

examples.input: arr=[5,6,7,8], calculate indices for start=2, steps=3

approaches.name: Greedy O(n)
approaches.summary: Check if total gas is at least total cost; if so, iterate through stations, tracking current gas balance, and reset the start index when balance goes negative. Efficiently finds the valid start in one pass.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Gas Station Complete Greedy Solution
ladders.desc: Implement the complete greedy algorithm to find the starting gas station index for a circular route.
ladders.explanation: The gas station problem requires finding a starting index in a circular route where you can travel to all stations without running out of gas. The greedy O(n) approach works by first checking if total gas is at least total cost—if not, return -1 immediately since no solution exists. If total gas is sufficient, we iterate through stations while maintaining a current gas balance. The key insight is that if at any point the current balance becomes negative, we can reset the start index to the next station and reset the balance to zero. This works because any segment with negative net gas cannot be part of a valid starting segment. The algorithm completes in one pass, making it efficient. Intuition: Think of the route as having segments where gas surplus (gas[i]-cost[i]) accumulates; we're looking for a starting point where the cumulative surplus never drops below zero when traversing the entire circle. The reset mechanism efficiently discards invalid starting candidates. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize total_gas=0, total_cost=0, current_gas=0, start=0. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Add gas[i] to total_gas and cost[i] to total_cost. b. Update current_gas += gas[i]-cost[i]. c. If current_gas < 0: set start = i+1 and current_gas = 0. 3. After loop, if total_gas < total_cost: return -1 else return start. Time: O(n) for single pass. Space: O(1) using only a few variables. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of tracking and resetting current gas. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check total gas vs cost, incorrectly handling circular wrap-around (algorithm inherently handles it), or off-by-one errors in start index. Real code pattern: def canCompleteCircuit(gas, cost): total_gas = total_cost = current_gas = start = 0; for i in range(len(gas)): total_gas += gas[i]; total_cost += cost[i]; current_gas += gas[i] - cost[i]; if current_gas < 0: start = i+1; current_gas = 0; return start if total_gas >= total_cost else -1.

examples.trace: Initialize total_gas=0, total_cost=0, current_gas=0, start=0. i=0: gas[0]=1, cost[0]=3, diff=-2. total_gas=1, total_cost=3, current_gas=-2 (<0) → reset start=1, current_gas=0. i=1: gas[1]=2, cost[1]=4, diff=-2. total_gas=3, total_cost=7, current_gas=-2 (<0) → reset start=2, current_gas=0. i=2: gas[2]=3, cost[2]=5, diff=-2. total_gas=6, total_cost=12, current_gas=-2 (<0) → reset start=3, current_gas=0. i=3: gas[3]=4, cost[3]=1, diff=+3. total_gas=10, total_cost=13, current_gas=3. i=4: gas[4]=5, cost[4]=2, diff=+3. total_gas=15, total_cost=15, current_gas=6. Loop ends: total_gas=15 >= total_cost=15 → return start=3.

Initialize total_gas=0, total_cost=0, current_gas=0, start=0. i=0: gas[0]=2, cost[0]=3, diff=-1. total_gas=2, total_cost=3, current_gas=-1 (<0) → reset start=1, current_gas=0. i=1: gas[1]=3, cost[1]=4, diff=-1. total_gas=5, total_cost=7, current_gas=-1 (<0) → reset start=2, current_gas=0. i=2: gas[2]=4, cost[2]=3, diff=+1. total_gas=9, total_cost=10, current_gas=1. Loop ends: total_gas=9 < total_cost=10 → return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Track and Reset Current Gas Balance
ladders.desc: Implement the core iteration that tracks current gas balance and resets start index when balance goes negative.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the greedy algorithm. Given arrays gas and cost, we iterate through indices while maintaining a running balance (current_gas) of gas[i]-cost[i]. Whenever current_gas becomes negative, we reset the start index to i+1 and set current_gas back to 0. Intuition: The current_gas represents the net gas available from the current candidate start index to the current station. If it goes negative, it means starting from the current candidate is impossible to reach station i+1, so we discard that candidate and try the next station as a new start. This works because any segment with cumulative negative net gas cannot be part of a valid solution—there must be a fresh start after it. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize current_gas=0, start=0. 2. For i from 0 to n-1: a. Update current_gas += gas[i]-cost[i]. b. If current_gas < 0: set start = i+1 and current_gas = 0. 3. Return start. Note: This alone doesn't guarantee a valid circuit; it needs the total gas check from L0. Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is embedded in the full solution to efficiently find the candidate start. Connection to L2: It relies on the insight that negative segments can be skipped. Common mistakes: Not resetting current_gas to 0 after negative balance, or incorrectly updating start (should be i+1, not i). Real code pattern: def find_candidate_start(gas, cost): current_gas = start = 0; for i in range(len(gas)): current_gas += gas[i] - cost[i]; if current_gas < 0: start = i+1; current_gas = 0; return start.

examples.trace: Initialize current_gas=0, start=0. i=0: diff=1-3=-2, current_gas=-2 (<0) → start=1, current_gas=0. i=1: diff=2-4=-2, current_gas=-2 (<0) → start=2, current_gas=0. i=2: diff=3-5=-2, current_gas=-2 (<0) → start=3, current_gas=0. i=3: diff=4-1=+3, current_gas=3. i=4: diff=5-2=+3, current_gas=6. Return start=3.

Initialize current_gas=0, start=0. i=0: diff=2-3=-1, current_gas=-1 (<0) → start=1, current_gas=0. i=1: diff=3-4=-1, current_gas=-1 (<0) → start=2, current_gas=0. i=2: diff=4-3=+1, current_gas=1. Return start=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Reset on Negative Cumulative Sum
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the logic to reset start index when cumulative gas balance goes negative.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the key insight: if the cumulative sum of (gas[i]-cost[i]) from a candidate start index becomes negative at some point, that candidate cannot be a valid start. Therefore, we reset to try the next index as a new start. Intuition: Imagine driving from a start station; if at any station you have negative gas, you couldn't have started there. By resetting, we efficiently skip over invalid segments. The cumulative sum represents the net gas accumulated since the last reset. When it goes negative, it indicates that the segment from the current start to the current station has a deficit, so we discard it and start fresh from the next station. This is greedy because we make a local decision (reset) that leads to a global optimum. Step-by-step: Given an array of differences diff[i]=gas[i]-cost[i], maintain a running sum. Whenever sum < 0, reset sum to 0 and note the next index as potential start. This logic assumes we will later check total gas (from L0). Time: O(n). Space: O(1). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the iteration loop. Connection to L3: It builds on computing differences between gas and cost. Common mistakes: Resetting sum to 0 but not updating start correctly, or not handling the reset condition properly. Real code pattern: def reset_on_negative(diffs): total = 0; start = 0; for i in range(len(diffs)): total += diffs[i]; if total < 0: start = i+1; total = 0; return start.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0, start=0. i=0: total=-2 (<0) → start=1, total=0. i=1: total=-2 (<0) → start=2, total=0. i=2: total=-2 (<0) → start=3, total=0. i=3: total=3. i=4: total=6. Return start=3.
Initialize total=0, start=0. i=0: total=-1 (<0) → start=1, total=0. i=1: total=-1 (<0) → start=2, total=0. i=2: total=1. Return start=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Gas-Cost Differences
ladders.desc: Calculate the difference between gas available and cost to travel for each station.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves computing an array where each element is the net gas at a station: diff[i] = gas[i] - cost[i]. Intuition: This difference represents the surplus or deficit of gas when moving from station i to i+1. A positive diff means you gain gas, negative means you consume more than available. This array simplifies the problem because we only need to consider these net values when determining if a circular route is possible. The cumulative sum of diffs from a start index must remain non-negative for a valid circuit. Step-by-step: Given two arrays gas and cost of length n, create a new array diff of length n where for each i, diff[i] = gas[i] - cost[i]. This is a straightforward element-wise subtraction. Time: O(n) to iterate through arrays. Space: O(n) if storing the array, but can be O(1) if computed on the fly in higher levels. Connection to L2: The diff array is used in the cumulative sum logic. Connection to L4: It relies on basic arithmetic subtraction. Common mistakes: Mismatching array lengths, incorrect indexing, or not handling negative values properly. Real code pattern: def compute_diffs(gas, cost): diffs = []; for i in range(len(gas)): diffs.append(gas[i] - cost[i]); return diffs.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate and Accumulate Array Sum
ladders.desc: Loop through an array and compute a running total (cumulative sum).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves iterating over an array and maintaining a cumulative sum of its elements. Intuition: Many algorithms require tracking the sum of elements up to a certain point, which helps in making decisions based on accumulated values. For example, in the gas station problem, we accumulate the differences (gas-cost) to check if the net gas ever goes negative. Step-by-step: Initialize a variable total = 0. For each element in the array, add it to total. This can be done with a simple loop. Time: O(n) for n elements. Space: O(1) for the total variable. Connection to L3: This operation is used to compute the cumulative sum of diffs. Connection to L5: It builds on basic loop and arithmetic concepts. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize total, incorrect loop bounds, or not updating total correctly. Real code pattern: def accumulate(arr): total = 0; for num in arr: total += num; return total.

examples.trace: Initialize total=0. Add 1 → total=1. Add 2 → total=3. Add 3 → total=6. Add 4 → total=10. Add 5 → total=15. Return 15.
Initialize total=0. Add -2 → total=-2. Add -2 → total=-4. Add -2 → total=-6. Add 3 → total=-3. Add 3 → total=0. Return 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal with a Loop
ladders.desc: Learn to iterate through an array using a loop and access each element.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: traversing an array (or list) using a loop. Intuition: Arrays store multiple values, and we often need to process each value one by one. A loop allows us to repeat an action for each element without writing redundant code. In programming, this is typically done with a for-loop or while-loop. Step-by-step: Given an array arr of length n, use a loop that runs from index 0 to n-1. In each iteration, access the element at the current index (e.g., arr[i]) and perform an operation like printing or adding to a sum. Time: O(n) since we visit each element once. Space: O(1) if no extra data structures are used. Connection to L4: This foundational skill is needed to accumulate sums or compute differences. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., going beyond array bounds), incorrect loop initialization, or misunderstanding how indices work. Real code pattern: def traverse(arr): for i in range(len(arr)): element = arr[i]; # do something with element.

examples.trace: Initialize loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: access arr[0]=10, print 10. i=1: access arr[1]=20, print 20. i=2: access arr[2]=30, print 30. End loop.
Initialize loop i from 0 to 2. i=0: access arr[0]=5, print 5. i=1: access arr[1]=-3, print -3. i=2: access arr[2]=2, print 2. End loop.

approaches.name: Prefix Sum O(n)
approaches.summary: Compute prefix sums of (gas - cost) to find the minimum cumulative deficit; the next index after the minimum is the starting station. Uses extra space to store sums for clarity and debugging.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Gas Station with Prefix Sum Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find starting gas station index using prefix sums of (gas - cost).
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires determining if there's a starting gas station index that allows completing a circular route, given arrays of gas available at each station and cost to travel to the next station. The prefix sum approach works by first calculating the net gain/loss at each station as gas[i] - cost[i]. If the total sum of all net values is negative, it's impossible to complete the circuit. Otherwise, we compute prefix sums of these net values. The key insight is that the starting station should be the index immediately after the minimum prefix sum point. This works because the minimum prefix sum represents the maximum cumulative deficit encountered during the journey. Starting after this point ensures we begin with enough cumulative surplus to overcome that deficit. The algorithm: 1) Calculate net = gas[i] - cost[i] for all i. 2) If sum(net) < 0, return -1 (impossible). 3) Initialize prefix_sum = 0 and min_prefix = 0, min_index = 0. 4) Iterate through net array: update prefix_sum += net[i]; if prefix_sum < min_prefix: update min_prefix = prefix_sum and min_index = i. 5) Return (min_index + 1) % n. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass through arrays. Space complexity is O(n) if we store the net array, or O(1) if we compute on the fly, but the approach described uses O(n) for clarity. Connection to L1: This uses the prefix sum computation as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to check total sum first, off-by-one errors in index calculation, and not handling the circular nature properly. The intuition: We're looking for the point where cumulative fuel never drops below zero when starting from that station, which corresponds to starting after the deepest deficit.

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate net = gas[i] - cost[i] = [1-3, 2-4, 3-5, 4-1, 5-2] = [-2, -2, -2, 3, 3]. Step 2: Total sum = -2 + -2 + -2 + 3 + 3 = 0 (≥ 0, possible). Step 3: Initialize prefix_sum = 0, min_prefix = 0, min_index = 0. Step 4: Iterate: i=0: prefix_sum = -2, min_prefix = -2, min_index = 0. i=1: prefix_sum = -4, min_prefix = -4, min_index = 1. i=2: prefix_sum = -6, min_prefix = -6, min_index = 2. i=3: prefix_sum = -3, min_prefix = -6 (no change). i=4: prefix_sum = 0, min_prefix = -6 (no change). Step 5: Starting index = (min_index + 1) % 5 = (2 + 1) % 5 = 3. Output: 3.
Step 1: Calculate net = [2-3, 3-4, 4-3] = [-1, -1, 1]. Step 2: Total sum = -1 + -1 + 1 = -1 (< 0, impossible). Step 3: Return -1 immediately. Output: -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compute Prefix Sum and Find Minimum
ladders.desc: Calculate prefix sums of an array and track the minimum value and its index.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core computational step in the gas station problem. Given an array of integers (representing net gas values), we compute the running cumulative sum (prefix sum) as we iterate through the array. Simultaneously, we track the minimum prefix sum encountered and the index where this minimum occurs. The intuition is that the prefix sum represents the cumulative net gas from the start to the current point. The minimum prefix sum indicates the point of maximum deficit. In the context of the gas station problem, starting after this minimum point ensures we begin with enough fuel to cover the worst deficit. Algorithm: 1) Initialize prefix_sum = 0, min_prefix = 0, min_index = 0. 2) For each index i from 0 to n-1: a) Update prefix_sum += array[i]. b) If prefix_sum < min_prefix: set min_prefix = prefix_sum and min_index = i. 3) Return min_prefix and min_index. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. Connection to L0: This subroutine directly provides the min_index used to compute the starting station. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of tracking cumulative sums. Common mistakes include initializing min_prefix incorrectly (should be 0 to represent starting point), not updating min_index properly, or off-by-one errors in index handling. The intuition: By finding the lowest point in the cumulative journey, we identify where the journey is most difficult, and starting just after that point gives us the best chance.

examples.trace: Initialize: prefix_sum = 0, min_prefix = 0, min_index = 0. i=0: prefix_sum = -2, min_prefix = -2, min_index = 0. i=1: prefix_sum = -4, min_prefix = -4, min_index = 1. i=2: prefix_sum = -6, min_prefix = -6, min_index = 2. i=3: prefix_sum = -3, min_prefix = -6 (no change). i=4: prefix_sum = 0, min_prefix = -6 (no change). Output: min_prefix = -6, min_index = 2.

Initialize: prefix_sum = 0, min_prefix = 0, min_index = 0. i=0: prefix_sum = 1, min_prefix = 0 (no change). i=1: prefix_sum = 0, min_prefix = 0 (no change). i=2: prefix_sum = 2, min_prefix = 0 (no change). i=3: prefix_sum = -1, min_prefix = -1, min_index = 3. i=4: prefix_sum = 3, min_prefix = -1 (no change). Output: min_prefix = -1, min_index = 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Cumulative Sum and Minimum Tracking
ladders.desc: Understand how tracking cumulative sums and their minimum helps find optimal starting points.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the conceptual insight behind using cumulative sums and their minimum. In many problems involving sequences or circular routes, we need to find a starting point where some cumulative measure never falls below zero. The core logic is: by computing the cumulative sum (prefix sum) of a sequence, we can visualize the 'journey' as a path that goes up and down. The minimum point on this path represents the deepest deficit. If we start our journey just after this minimum point, we begin at a higher point relative to the minimum, ensuring that throughout the circular journey, the cumulative sum never drops below the starting level. Mathematically, for a circular array, starting at index k means we consider the sequence rotated. The condition for feasibility is that for all j, the sum from k to j (modulo n) is non-negative. By tracking the prefix sum from the original order and finding its minimum, we can derive k as (min_index + 1) mod n. The intuition: Imagine the cumulative sum as a graph; the minimum is the lowest valley. Starting after the valley means we begin climbing from a point above the valley floor, so we never go below it again. This logic applies to problems like gas station, but also to scheduling, resource allocation, etc. Time and space are O(n) for computation, but the insight is key. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the prefix sum subroutine. Connection to L3: It builds on understanding prefix sums. Common mistakes include misinterpreting the minimum index or not considering circular wrap-around. The step-by-step: 1) Compute sequence of values (e.g., net gas). 2) Calculate cumulative sums. 3) Find min cumulative sum and its index. 4) Starting point is next index. Why it works: Because the total sum is non-negative, the cumulative sum after the min will always be sufficient to cover earlier deficits when starting from there.

examples.trace: Cumulative sums: start at 0, then -2, -4, -6, -3, 0. Minimum is -6 at index 2. Since total sum = 0, feasible. Starting after index 2 means index 3. Check: From index 3: cumulative from 3 onward: 3, 3+3=6, then wrap to -2 (6-2=4), -2 (4-2=2), -2 (2-2=0). All ≥0. Logic holds.

Cumulative sums: 0, 1, 0, 2, -1, 3. Minimum is -1 at index 3. Total sum = 3 ≥0. Start after index 3 means index 4. Check from index 4: 4, wrap to 1 (4+1=5), -1 (5-1=4), 2 (4+2=6), -3 (6-3=3). All ≥0. Works.

ladders.title: Building Block — Prefix Sum Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the prefix sum array from a given array of numbers.
ladders.explanation: A prefix sum is a cumulative sum of elements in an array up to each index. It's a fundamental building block in algorithm design, used for efficient range sum queries, data analysis, and problems like gas station. Given an array arr of length n, the prefix sum array prefix is defined as prefix[i] = sum of arr[0] to arr[i] for i from 0 to n-1. Often, we compute it iteratively: start with prefix[0] = arr[0], then for i from 1 to n-1, prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]. Alternatively, we can compute in-place or use a running sum variable. The intuition is that prefix sums transform a sequence into its cumulative totals, allowing us to quickly compute sums of any subarray: sum from i to j = prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] (with care for i=0). In the gas station problem, we use prefix sums of net gas values to track cumulative fuel. Time complexity is O(n) to compute, and space is O(n) if storing the array, or O(1) if using a running sum. Connection to L2: Prefix sums are the data structure used to implement the cumulative logic. Connection to L4: It involves basic addition operations. Common mistakes include index errors, especially when accessing prefix[i-1] for i=0, or misunderstanding that prefix sums can be negative. The algorithm: 1) Initialize an array prefix of size n. 2) Set prefix[0] = arr[0]. 3) For i from 1 to n-1: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i]. 4) Return prefix. For gas station, we might compute on the fly without storing all values. Real code pattern: prefix = [0]*n; prefix[0] = arr[0]; for i in range(1,n): prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + arr[i].

examples.trace: Step 1: prefix[0] = arr[0] = 1. Step 2: i=1: prefix[1] = prefix[0] + arr[1] = 1 + 2 = 3. i=2: prefix[2] = prefix[1] + arr[2] = 3 + 3 = 6. i=3: prefix[3] = prefix[2] + arr[3] = 6 + 4 = 10. i=4: prefix[4] = prefix[3] + arr[4] = 10 + 5 = 15. Output: [1,3,6,10,15].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element-wise Subtraction and Summation
ladders.desc: Subtract corresponding elements of two arrays and compute the sum of an array.
ladders.explanation: This level covers fundamental array operations used as building blocks in the gas station problem. First, element-wise subtraction: given two arrays of equal length, gas and cost, we compute a new array net where net[i] = gas[i] - cost[i] for each index i. This represents the net gain or loss of fuel at each station after traveling to the next. Second, summation: given an array, compute the total sum of all its elements. This is used to check if the total net gas is non-negative, which is a necessary condition for completing the circuit. The intuition is simple arithmetic: we need to know if overall, there's enough gas to cover total cost. These operations are basic but critical; errors here can propagate. Algorithm for subtraction: 1) Check gas and cost have same length n. 2) Initialize net array of size n. 3) For i from 0 to n-1: net[i] = gas[i] - cost[i]. 4) Return net. For summation: 1) Initialize total = 0. 2) For each element in array: total += element. 3) Return total. Time complexity is O(n) for both, space is O(n) for subtraction if storing net, or O(1) for summation. Connection to L3: These operations produce the input for prefix sum computation. Connection to L5: They rely on basic arithmetic and loops. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds, integer overflow (though rare in problems), or incorrect subtraction order. Real code pattern: net = [gas[i] - cost[i] for i in range(n)]; total = sum(net). The subtraction gives per-station net, and the sum gives global feasibility check.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand arrays, indexing, and simple arithmetic operations.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational level for beginners. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index. In programming, arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. For example, an array gas = [1,2,3] has gas[0] = 1, gas[1] = 2, gas[2] = 3. Basic arithmetic operations include addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. In the context of the gas station problem, we primarily use subtraction to compute net values and addition to compute sums. The intuition is straightforward: we manipulate numbers stored in arrays to solve problems. To access an element, use the index: value = array[i]. To iterate through an array, use a loop from i=0 to length-1. For two arrays of the same length, we can perform element-wise operations by accessing corresponding indices. This foundation is essential because all higher levels build on these concepts. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's basic. Connection to L4: These operations are used in element-wise subtraction and summation. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops, confusing indices, or incorrect arithmetic order. Step-by-step: 1) Declare arrays. 2) Use a for loop to iterate. 3) Inside loop, perform arithmetic like subtraction. 4) Store or output results. Real code pattern in Python-style: for i in range(len(array)): do something with array[i]. Example: compute net[i] = gas[i] - cost[i]. This level assumes no prior algorithm knowledge, just basic programming.

examples.trace: Array gas has indices 0 and 1: gas[0]=5, gas[1]=3. Array cost: cost[0]=2, cost[1]=4. For i=0: net[0] = gas[0] - cost[0] = 5 - 2 = 3. For i=1: net[1] = gas[1] - cost[1] = 3 - 4 = -1. So net array would be [3, -1].
Array arr has indices 0,1,2: arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3. Initialize sum = 0. For i=0: sum += arr[0] = 0+1=1. i=1: sum += arr[1] = 1+2=3. i=2: sum += arr[2] = 3+3=6. Final sum = 6.
